coredata.dc:title: Preparation and evaluation of a double-hydrophilic interaction stationary phase based on bovine serum albumin and graphene quantum dots modified silica
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Qiurong
Wan, Meijun
Zhou, Jingqiu
Dai, Xuemei
Yang, Honglin
Zu, Fengjiao
Zheng, Yunchao
Wang, Lujun
coredata.dc:description: The bovine serum albumin (BSA) was firstly bonded on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized silica as the stationary phase named as BSA@GQDs@SiO2 which can perform hydrophilic interaction between the stationary phase and analytes. Characteristic methods including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), elemental analysis (EA), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) were applied to estimate the chemical bonding results and morphological features of the prepared materials. In the chromatographic evaluation part, the BSA@GQDs@SiO2 column showed excellent separation efficiency for the analysis of hydrophilic compounds involving nucleosides and bases, acids, phenols, quinolones, vitamins and alkaloids, which proved the prepared column can exhibit hydrophilic interaction mode with other interactions including hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction. What's more, essential experimental approaches were designed to evaluate the retention properties of the prepared column via comparing with a commercial amino column and a prepared GQDs@SiO2 column. Results indicated the prepared column showed excellent separation ability for different kinds of hydrophilic compounds than commercial and single hydrophilic columns. The study showed that BSA@GQDs@SiO2 stationary phase with double hydrophilic materials will be a potential chromatographic material for analyzing various hydrophilic analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Bovine serum albumin
Hydrophilic interaction
Chromatographic separation
Stationary phase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis of stationary phase and packing of column
Retention mechanism of the prepared column

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic route of the stationary phase material of BSA@GQDs@SiO2.
FT-IR spectra of SiO2, APTES@SiO2, GQDs@SiO2 and BSA@GQDs@SiO2 (A) and TGA curves of SiO2, APTES@SiO2, GQDs@SiO2 and BSA@GQDs@SiO2 (B).

Comparison of the chromatographic separation of alkaloids (A), quinolones (B), nucleosides and bases (C), acids (D) on BSA@GQDs@SiO2 and NH2 column. Peaks: A: (1) evodiamine, (2) berberine, (3) theophylline, (4) theobromine; B: (1) enrofloxacin, (2) pefloxacin, (3) lomefloxacin, (4) norfloxacin; C: (1) thiourea, (2) thymidine, (3) deoxyadenosine, (4) uridine, (5) cytosine, (6) cytidine; D: (1) salicylamide, (2) salicylic acid, (3) 3-aminosalicylic acid, (4) benzoic acid, (5) p-hydroxybenzoic acid, (6) 3-hydroxybenzoic acid.

Comparison of the chromatographic separation of alkaloids (A), quinolones (B), nucleosides and bases (C), acids (D) on BSA@GQDs@SiO2 and GQDs@SiO2 column. Peaks: A: (1) evodiamine, (2) berberine, (3) theophylline, (4) theobromine; B: (1) enrofloxacin, (2) pefloxacin, (3) lomefloxacin, (4) norfloxacin; C: (1) thiourea, (2) thymidine, (3) deoxyadenosine, (4) uridine, (5) cytosine, (6) cytidine; D: (1) salicylamide, (2) salicylic acid, (3) 3-Aminosalicylic acid, (4) benzoic acid, (5) p-hydroxybenzoic acid, (6) 3-hydroxybenzoic acid.
Retention trend diagrams of phenols (A), nucleosides and bases (B), quinolones (C) and acids (D) versus the ACN proportion in the mobile phase.

Separation of (1) Vitamin B1, (2) Nicotinamide, (3) Pyridoxine, (4) Riboflavin with BSA@GQDs@SiO2 column (A). Retention trend diagrams versus the ACN proportion in the mobile phase (B). Comparison of the chromatographic separation of phenols on BSA@GQDs@SiO2 and NH2 column (C). Peaks: (1) o-nitrophenol; (2) p-nitrophenol, (3) pyrogallol.
Elemental analysis data of SiO2, APTES@SiO2, GQDs@SiO2 and BSA@GQDs@SiO2.
Theoretical plate numbers (N/m) of nucleosides and bases on BSA@GQDs@SiO2 and commercial NH2 columns.

head.ce:title: Preparation and evaluation of a double-hydrophilic interaction stationary phase based on bovine serum albumin and graphene quantum dots modified silica

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nursing, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Nursing, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The bovine serum albumin (BSA) was firstly bonded on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized silica as the stationary phase named as BSA@GQDs@SiO2 which can perform hydrophilic interaction between the stationary phase and analytes. Characteristic methods including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), elemental analysis (EA), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) were applied to estimate the chemical bonding results and morphological features of the prepared materials. In the chromatographic evaluation part, the BSA@GQDs@SiO2 column showed excellent separation efficiency for the analysis of hydrophilic compounds involving nucleosides and bases, acids, phenols, quinolones, vitamins and alkaloids, which proved the prepared column can exhibit hydrophilic interaction mode with other interactions including hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction. What's more, essential experimental approaches were designed to evaluate the retention properties of the prepared column via comparing with a commercial amino column and a prepared GQDs@SiO2 column. Results indicated the prepared column showed excellent separation ability for different kinds of hydrophilic compounds than commercial and single hydrophilic columns. The study showed that BSA@GQDs@SiO2 stationary phase with double hydrophilic materials will be a potential chromatographic material for analyzing various hydrophilic analytes.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromatographic stationary phase is considered as the core component of high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). Researching the stationary phase materials is playing an important role and attracts attentions of scholars in the field of separation science. Among different kinds of chromatographic modes, reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) is widely applied in various fields by separation scientists. However, RPLC is facing some challenges due to the difficulty in the separation of high polar substances [1]. In order to remedy the deficiency of RPLC, hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC) has become one of the fastest developed chromatographic mode, providing another preferred route for the separation and analysis of high polar compounds, especially for pharmaceutical analysis [2–4]. Alpert was firstly designed various polar groups modified silica as HILIC packing materials in 1990 [5]. Most of the drug candidates, metabolites, biomarkers possess strong polar properties, facing some difficulties for analysis on the RPLC column due to their weak retention. Fortunately, hydrophilic chromatography is exhibiting some advantages to solve these problems and becoming an ideal tool for analyzing and separating strong polar substances such as polypeptides and amino acids [6–8]. Hence, researching the packing materials of hydrophilic chromatography has become a hotspot in the field of separation science and will promote the development of related fields.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is attracting widely attentions due to the characteristics of high surface area, activated surface groups, good water dispersion and excellent adsorption performance [9], and has made some breakthroughs in separation field including solid phase extraction (SPE) [10], capillary electric chromatography (CEC) [11], gas chromatography (GC) [12] and liquid chromatography (LC) [13]. GQDs possesses highly delocalized conjugate system of π-electron and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxy and carboxyl, giving it multifunctional properties including hydrophilic interaction, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding properties. To date, GQDs has been successfully applied as a modification material to synthesize HPLC stationary phases [14]. Some groups have utilized GQDs modified silica as novel separation materials for performing various separation modes in HPLC. For example, Qiu's group used GQDs modified silica as HPLC stationary phase, which indicated that the novel column could achieve multiple separation modes for separation of alkaloids, nucleosides and anilines, etc [15]. Recently, the group have designed different kinds of GQDs-based stationary phase and achieved the separation of various compounds [16–18]. Our group modified GQDs and poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAAm) onto the silica substrate for performing RPLC/HILIC mixed-mode stationary phase, and successfully applied it to determine hydrophilic ingredients in banlangen granules [19]. According to these research, GQDs-bonded silica stationary phase showed mixed retention mechanism with analytes including hydrophilic interaction, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding et al [20,21]. Hence, exploring novel GQDs materials, especially GQDs based composite materials as HPLC stationary phase is considered as an interesting topic in analytical fields.

Amino acid or poly-amino acid have been considered as a kind of important hydrophilic material, which is attracting attentions of separation scientists to prepare HILIC column for the analysis of hydrophilic compounds [22–25]. Serum albumin, the major protein in the circulating system, is a cardiac globular protein composed of 585 amino acids with a molecular weight about 66 kDa. Bovine serum albumin is one of the main available albumins which widely used in biomedical and pharmaceutical field [26,27]. To date, few BSA based HILIC columns were prepared to separate hydrophilic analytes. Huang et al. used cross-linked BSA as hydrophilic out layers for chromatographic analysis of enrofloxacin and gatifloxacin in a complex separation system [28]. However, the combination of GQDs and BSA for the construction of HILIC column is hitherto unknown.

Herein, BSA monomer was covalently bonded onto the surface of GQDs-based silica to prepare a novel double-HILIC stationary phase possessing better separating function for the analysis of hydrophilic compounds. Six kinds of polar compounds (acids, phenols, nucleosides and bases, quinolones, vitamins and alkaloids) were utilized to investigate the chromatographic performance of the prepared BSA@GQDs@SiO2 column. The prepared column showed better separation selectivity than commercial amino column (NH2@SiO2) and the GQDs@SiO2 column. These results indicated that the composite of two functional hydrophilic materials GQDs and BSA showed synergistic effect to separate hydrophilic substances. The retention mechanism was further explored by studying effects of retention of analytes varying with different constitutions of mobile phase.

Silica as the matrix (diameter 5.5 µm, pore size 100 Å, and specific surface area 465 m2 g −1) was obtained from Dasio Chemicals (Osaka, Japan). Graphite powder used to prepare GQDs was acquired from Lanmu Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) and three classes of substances used for chromatographic analysis including nucleosides/nucleobases (thiourea, thymidine, deoxyadenosine, uridine, cytosine, cytidine), phenols (o-nitrophenol, p-nitrophenol, pyrogallol), quinolones (enrofloxacin, pefloxacin, lomefloxacin, norfloxacin) and benzoic acids (p-hydroxybenzoic, salicylamide, salicylic acid, 3-aminosalicylic acid, 3-hydroxybenzoic acid, benzoic acid) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Vitamin B1 was bought from Innochem Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Nicotinamide was supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Vitamin B-class substances (pyridoxine and riboflavin) were provided by Ark Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Thiourea was supplied by General Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the purity of compounds used for the chromatographic analysis were greater than 98%. The commercial ZORBAX NH2 (4.6 × 150 mm i.d., 5 µm) column used to compare was purchased from Agilent Technologies (California, USA).

A Vario EL III elemental analyzer was employed to analyze the element constitution of synthesized materials (Elementar, Germany). The characteristic functional groups were measured on IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was implemented on STA 449 F3 system (Netzsch, Germany). A UV-2600 UV–visible spectrophotometer was used to measure the ultraviolet absorption of the synthesized GQDs (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence characteristics of the GQDs were measured by an LS55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). A SU8010 high resolution field emission scanning electron microscope was used to determine the morphological features of the synthetic materials (Hitachi, Japan). A stainless steel column (4.6 × 150 mm i.d., 5.5 µm) used for packing the synthetic materials was bought from Dalian replete Science and Technology Co., Ltd (Dalian, China). All chromatographic experiments were performed on Prominence-I LC-2030 series (Shimadzu, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of stationary phase and packing of column
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, GQDs were prepared via a solvothermal method according to our previous work [29]. The route to prepare BSA@GQDs@SiO2 stationary phase was shown in Fig. 1 . In details, 2.7 g activated silica was poured into 100 mL dry toluene. Lately, 2 mL APTES was added into the mixture with injector and kept stirring at 100 °C for 24 h under the protection of nitrogen gas. Then the product APTES@SiO2 was separated by filtering and washing several times with acetone and methanol, finally dried overnight at 60 °C. Next, 100 mL GQDs aqueous dispersion (3 mg/mL) mixed with 0.3 g EDC and 0.2 g NHS was stirred for 0.5 h to activate carboxyl groups. After that, 2.5 g amino-modified silica was dispersed into the mixture and stirred for 24 h to obtain the product GQDs@SiO2. Lastly, BSA bonded GQDs@SiO2 was prepared as follows: 2.3 g GQDs@SiO2, 0.3 g EDC and 0.2 g NHS were put into a round-bottom flask and 70 mL 0.01 M phosphate buffer was used for dispersion (pH 5). After stirring for 0.5 h, 2.3 g BSA was added into the mixture with ultrasonication for 2–3 min and continued stirring for 24 h. Finally, the composites named as BSA@GQDs@SiO2 were washed with 0.01 M phosphate buffer, water and methanol by filtration and dried at 60 °C in vacuum oven.

Slurry packing method was applied to prepare the novel HPLC column. Firstly, BSA@GQDs@SiO2 material (2.0 g) was dispersed in the mixture of isopropanol/acetonitrile solution (v/v, 1/1, 10 mL). Then, the suspension liquid was poured into a stainless steel column using methanol as pushing agent. Finally, the prepared column was activated with methanol and water for 5 h on the HPLC apparatus before next chromatographic experiments.

The prepared GQDs was characterized firstly by FT-IR spectrophotometer, UV–visible spectrophotometer and luminescence spectrometer. As shown in Fig. S1A, the wide absorption band at 3412 cm−1 was the peak of OH, and the characteristic peak at 1601 cm−1 attributed to C = O of the carboxyl groups. The sharp absorption peak located at 1392 cm−1 primarily corresponded to the COH and the absorption peak at 1072 cm−1 belonged to CO. Furthermore, the UV–visible absorption spectra of GQDs showed pronounced absorption peak at 300 nm (Fig. S1B). The GQDs solution exhibited strong blue fluorescence at 365 nm UV light excitation and the optional excitation and emission wavelengths were 472 nm and 529 nm, respectively. All of the above results fitted the characteristics of GQDs [30,31].

The stationary phase materials of chemical compositions were then characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectra of APTES@SiO2, GQDs@SiO2 and BSA@GQDs@SiO2 were displayed in Fig. 2 A. Peak at 963 cm−1 belonged to Si-OH of silica, which weakened in curves of APTES@SiO2, GQDs@SiO2 and BSA@GQDs@SiO2. The result indicated that the silicon hydroxyl on the surface of silicon sphere was successfully modified. C=O band appeared at 1644 cm−1 suggested that GQDs were covalently bonded on the surface of silica. Besides, the amide I band and amide II band appeared on BSA@GQDs@SiO2 were assigned to the successful functionalization of BSA.

The morphological changes of APTES@SiO2, GQDs@SiO2 and BSA@GQDs@SiO2 were observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. S2, the surface of silica became rougher after the modification of GQDs and BSA. Moreover, TGA was used to characterize the thermal weight loss of modified functional materials on the surface of silica, which was performed up to 800 °C under N2 atmosphere. As shown in Fig. 2B, the mass loss observed from four curves was increased as the gradually increase of temperature, especially the BSA@GQDs@SiO2, that was because the silica achieved the highest degree of functionalization. However, GQDs is stable when the temperature reached to 800 °C, the curve of GQDs@SiO2 became abnormal and the weight loss is less than APTES@SiO2. According to these results, GQDs and BSA materials were successfully bonded onto the surface of silica matrix.

Furthermore, elemental analysis results (C, H and N) were revealed to show the element constitution of synthesized materials. As shown in Table 1 , the C content of 8.65% and N content of 2.01% from BSA@GQDs@SiO2 were obviously changed compared with the original material of bare silica, which demonstrated the variation of surface chemical state of the silica. Besides, the surface coverage (µ) of immobilized molecules was calculated based on the method depicted in our previous works [32–35]. Aminopropyl groups attached onto the silica was calculated as 3.86 µmol m− 2 based on the content of nitrogen, and BSA groups modified on the silica was calculated as 0.45 µmol m− 2. The calculation equations were described as follows:
μ
APTES
(
μ
mol
m
− 2
)
=
(
N 1
−
N 0
)
×
10
6
14 × S
,
μ
BSA
(
μ
mol
m
− 2
)
=
(
C 1
−
C 0
)
×
10
6
12 ×
S
where, C1 and C0 represent the percentage of carbon content from BSA@GQDs@SiO2 and GQDs@SiO2, N1 and N0 represent the percentage of nitrogen content from SiO2 and APTES@SiO2; S represents the specific surface area of silica.

Due to abundant hydrophilic groups such as amide and hydroxyl groups from materials of the GQDs and BSA, the prepared novel column can process hydrophilic interaction with analytes and show advantages for separating of polar compounds. Here, typical compounds including acids, phenols, nucleosides and bases, quinolones, vitamins and alkaloids were selected to evaluate the hydrophilic performance of the prepared column. According to the experimental results, the novel chromatographic column can elevate the analytic range compared with commercial amino column with only a single interaction force, which attributed that GQDs and BSA could provide more interaction forces beyond hydrophilic interaction, such as hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between hydroxyl groups and analytes. In addition, the GQDs@SiO2 column was prepared to compare with BSA@GQDs@SiO2. All compounds were evaluated in the same chromatographic conditions, including column temperature (30 °C), flow rate (1.0 mL/min), injection volume (10 µL) and UV detection wavelength (254 nm).

Fig. 3 showed the successful separation of alkaloids (A), quinolones (B), nucleosides and bases (C) and acids (D) on the prepared column. At the same time, separation of these compounds was also compared on commercial NH2 column (Fig. 3) and GQDs@SiO2 column (Fig. 4 ), but most compounds failed to be separated or showed worse resolution. In details, under the respective optimal conditions of mobile phase, four alkaloids were separated on the prepared BSA@GQDs@SiO2 column with mobile phase of ACN/20 mM ammonium formate (v/v, 92/8), while compounds of berberine, theophylline and theobromine showed no sign of separation on commercial NH2 column (Fig. 3A). Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 4A, berberine and theophylline failed to be separated on GQDs@SiO2 column. Besides, six acids were separated with mobile phase of ACN/20 mM ammonium formate (v/v, 85/15) on the prepared column but peaks of benzoic acid and p-hydroxybenzoic acid coincided with each other on NH2 column (Fig. 3D). The above results indicated that the composite material of GQDs and BSA endowed the novel stationary phase with better selectivity for the separation of hydrophilic compounds. In addition, compared with NH2 column and GQDs@SiO2 column, four quinolones obtained baseline separation under the mobile phase of ACN/20 mM ammonium formate (v/v, 87/13) on the novel column which showed in Fig. 3B and Fig. 4B. Besides, the peak shape of quinolones was improved compared with NH2 column. In terms of nucleosides and bases in Fig. 3C, though successful separation was realized on both prepared and commercial columns with mobile phase of ACN/20 mM ammonium formate (v/v, 95/15). However, the separation efficiency of novel column was better than commercial NH2 column and the theoretical plate numbers (N/m) of nucleosides and bases on these two columns listed in Table. 2 . In addition, cytosine and cytidine cannot realize baseline separation on the GQDs@SiO2 column (Fig. 4C). Finally, the prepared BSA@GQDs@SiO2 column has also been used to separate three phenols (o-nitrophenol, p-nitrophenol, pyrogallol) with mobile phase of ACN/20 mM ammonium formate (v/v, 94/6) shown in Fig. 6C, while p-nitrophenol and pyrogallol cannot achieve baseline separation on NH2 column. In addition, the mixture of water-soluble vitamins including vitamin B1, nicotinamide, pyridoxine and riboflavin was achieved baseline separation on the chromatographic condition of ACN/water (v/v, 81/19) in Fig. 6A. In a word, advantages of the prepared BSA@GQDs@SiO2 column were apparent, which showed a better separation selectivity for various kinds of hydrophilic compounds. These results indicated that the composite of two functional hydrophilic materials GQDs and BSA showed synergistic effect to separate hydrophilic substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Retention mechanism of the prepared column
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, the relationship between the elution order of analytes and their log p values was investigated. As shown in Table S1, the eluted order of quinolones and phenols was in consistent with their log P values. The eluted order of three alkaloids conformed to the log P value except for theophylline causing from the π-π conjugation between benzene ring of theophylline and the stationary phase. The substances of nucleosides and nucleobases were eluted according to log P value, while the cytidine displayed an exception owing to the presence of the hydrogen bond and π-π conjugation with the stationary phase [39]. For six acids in Fig. 5D, salicylamide, salicylic acid and 3-aminosalicylic acid eluted with the order of log P value. While the elution order of benzoic acid, p-hydroxybenzoic acid and 3-hydroxybenzoic acid was inconformity with their log P values due to other interaction between the stationary phase and analytes. In conclusion, the abnormal relationship between elution order and log P value can be contributed to multi-interactions, such as electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bond, between the prepared column and analytes except the typical hydrophilic retention mechanism.

A novel HILIC stationary phase named BSA@GQDs@SiO2 was designed by bonding BSA onto the surface of GQDs-modified silica via amidate reaction. Results of EA, FT-IR and TGA confirmed that GQDs and BSA were successfully modified on the surface of silica beads. In addition, six kinds of hydrophilic compounds were realized baseline separation on the prepared column. Retention mechanism was also investigated by researching the effect of water proportional varying with retention time of analytes. Especially, the prepared BSA@GQDs@SiO2 column performed better separation efficiency than commercial hydrophilic column and the GQDs@SiO2 column. In conclusion, combining of hydrophilic materials (GQDs and BSA) can be utilized as a meaningful composite ligand in chromatographic stationary phase for performing hydrophilic interaction with analytes.

Qiurong Luo: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Meijun Wan: Investigation, Resources. Jingqiu Zhou: Software, Visualization, Investigation. Xuemei Dai: Resources, Writing – original draft. Honglin Yang: Writing – original draft. Fengjiao Zu: Resources, Writing – original draft. Yunchao Zheng: Resources, Writing – original draft. Lujun Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21804112 and 21974015) and the talent introduction program of Southwest Medical University (No. 090300040040).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chroma.2022.462933.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel double polymer modified hydrophobic/hydrophilic stationary phase for liquid chromatography
Chin. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Fan, J. Pan, Y. Li, L. Wang, S. Wang, X. Liang, Y. Guo, A novel double polymer modified hydrophobic/hydrophilic stationary phase for liquid chromatography, Chin. Chem. Lett.31 (2020) 746–750.

J. Chromatogr. A

J. Chromatogr. A
